companyCaseStudy.title: How Netflix Built Open Connect: Serving Tens of Tbps from the Edge
companyCaseStudy.summary: Netflix delivers video to 200M+ members worldwide and at peak moves tens of terabits/sec. To avoid buying endless backbone capacity and to cut startup latency, Netflix built Open Connect — a global, ISP‑deployed CDN of caching appliances that serves the majority of traffic locally, reduces backbone egress, and dramatically improves playback performance.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

At global peak hours Netflix traffic represents a striking share of Internet downstream — in some countries a third of peak traffic. To handle that scale (tens of Tbps) and keep startup latency low for hundreds of millions of devices, Netflix built its own CDN: Open Connect.

---

### Problem: exploding bandwidth and unhappy customers

In the early 2010s Netflix saw two correlated problems: member growth (100M+ devices requesting video) drove exponential bandwidth needs, and delivering video from a small set of central datacenters meant high backbone egress cost and poor viewer startup times. Netflix needed to get terabits of traffic *closer to eyeballs* while retaining control of video quality and measurement.

Numbers to keep in mind:

- Netflix serves video for 200M+ subscribers (global footprint).
- Peak global throughput is in the order of tens of terabits/sec (multi‑Tbps) during high demand windows.
- Goal: serve the majority of requests from within ISP networks to cut backbone egress and reduce startup latency.

### Challenge: an owned, globally distributed CDN that fits ISP constraints

Constraints Netflix had to overcome:

- ISPs have widely varying policies, rack space, bandwidth capacity, and trust boundaries.
- Video is large — caches must be sized and placed to get high hit rates (target often >80–90% in deployed networks).
- Compatibility with many client devices and ABR (adaptive bitrate) workflows.
- Measurement and operational control across thousands of caches.

### Solution: Open Connect — appliances + smart caching + telemetry

Netflix created Open Connect: a fleet of Open Connect Appliances (OCAs) deployed inside ISP networks and at Internet exchange points, plus a global control system that places content, monitors cache health, and steers clients to the best cache.

Key design decisions:

- Push content to caches inside ISP networks (prefetch/populate popular titles proactively). This converts cross‑ISP egress to local delivery.
- Use appliances sized per ISP and per PoP (from small rack units to multi‑rack deployments) to match local demand and rack constraints.
- Implement sophisticated placement & eviction strategies to keep popular titles hot in each region.
- Make caching maximally transparent: clients request content via DNS/HTTP and are routed to the nearest/most appropriate OCA.
- Instrument everything: per‑cache metrics, hit rates, client startup times, and QoE signals feed a controller that adapts distribution.

### Architecture (visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Client Device (TV / Phone / Browser)] --> DNS[DNS / CDN Steering]
  DNS --> OCA[Open Connect Appliance (ISP Edge)]
  OCA -->|Serve from Cache| Client
  OCA -->|Miss -> Fetch| Origin[Netflix Origin / Regional Backbone]
  Origin --> Storage[(Blob Store / Object Storage)]
  Origin --> Encoding[Encoding / Packaging]
  OCA --> Telemetry[Monitoring & Analytics]
  Telemetry --> Controller[Placement Controller]
  Controller --> OCA
```

### Flow (simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Client requests title] --> B[DNS steers to local OCA]
  B --> C{OCA has segment?}
  C -->|Hit| D[Serve segment (low latency)]
  C -->|Miss| E[Fetch from Origin] --> D
  D --> F[Client ABR picks bitrate] --> G[Telemetry]
  G --> H[Controller adjusts placement/evictions]
```

### Before vs After (real impact numbers)

| Metric | Before (centralized delivery) | After (Open Connect) |
|--------|-------------------------------:|---------------------:|
| Backbone egress for Netflix traffic | Tens of Tbps across interconnects | Reduced by a majority—Netflix reports most traffic served from caches; many ISPs see >80–90% local hit rates |
| Peak global throughput to ISPs | Centralized origins driving huge cross‑ISP flows | Tens of Tbps delivered locally across thousands of OCAs (multi‑Tbps aggregated) |
| Median playback startup | 1–2+ seconds (varied by network) | Often reduced into the 300–700ms range for many users after local caching |
| Cache hit rate (deployed networks) | N/A (no edge caches) | Typical target and observed >80% — in many ISPs >90% |
| User QoE (buffering events) | Higher buffering/rebuffer | Significant reduction in rebuffer events and better ABR stability |

> Note: Netflix has published multiple posts and talks stating that Open Connect serves the majority of Netflix traffic from ISP caches, often exceeding 80–90% hit rates in deployed networks. Exact global aggregated Tbps vary by peak day and region, but the design objective is clear: move terabits of traffic from backbone to edge.

### Operational and engineering practices that made it work

- Appliance program: Netflix ships and operates appliances to ISPs to guarantee hardware and software behavior.
- Proactive content placement: frequently watched titles are pushed into OCAs before spikes (e.g., new episode drops), increasing hit rates.
- Per‑segment ABR support and cache-aware manifests: caches serve HTTP‑range requests/manifest fragments efficiently.
- Robust telemetry pipeline: low‑latency metrics feed placement decisions and allow fast responses to cache failures.
- Partnership model with ISPs: in many cases the OCA is colocated in ISP data centers or exchanges with business agreements that simplify traffic engineering.

### Results (business + technical)

- Vastly reduced cross‑ISP egress: Netflix avoids sending all traffic over long haul backbones, saving on transit and improving predictability.
- Improved QoE: startup latency and rebuffer events fall significantly for users served from local caches, improving retention and engagement.
- Scalable global operation: by treating the CDN as owned infrastructure, Netflix can scale into tens of Tbps aggregated delivered capacity without centralizing bottlenecks.

### Lessons learned (quick)

- Put heavy, repeatable traffic close to the user — video is the canonical use case for edge caching.
- Operational ownership (shipped appliances + software) beats trying to be a passive CDN customer when you need determinism at scale.
- You still need a global control plane: placement, telemetry, and routing steering are as important as the boxes themselves.

### Caveats

- Open Connect is capital and ops heavy: hardware procurement, shipping, software lifecycle, and global monitoring are nontrivial.
- The model relies on cooperation from ISPs; not every market will accept colocated appliances.

---

If you build large‑scale media delivery or any system with predictable, repeatable heavy payloads, Open Connect is a concrete example of moving from reactive transit purchases to proactive, owned edge delivery.
keyTakeaways: Edge-first delivery scales: pushing caches into ISP networks converted tens of Tbps of backbone traffic into local delivery, often achieving cache hit rates >80–90%.
Operational ownership matters: shipping and operating appliances + a control plane (placement, telemetry) lets Netflix guarantee QoE at huge scale.
Measure and prepopulate: proactive placement of popular titles and fine-grained telemetry were essential to reduce startup latency (often to <1s) and rebuffering.

concept.title: Cache Smarter: When to Use Cache‑Aside, Write‑Through, and Write‑Back
concept.summary: Caching reduces latency and load on your database, but the *how* matters. This guide explains the three core caching strategies (cache‑aside, write‑through, write‑back) with analogies, diagrams, code, before/after comparisons, and common pitfalls so you can pick the right approach for your use case.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Databases are slower and more expensive than memory. Uncached traffic can overwhelm DBs and cause bad user experiences: slow pages, timeouts, and high cloud costs. Caching makes reads fast and reduces DB QPS — but done wrong it introduces stale data, race conditions, and extra complexity.

Think of your system like a busy café:
- The database is the kitchen that prepares every meal.
- The cache is a fridge at the counter with prepped grab‑and‑go meals.
- Different caching strategies are how the staff keep the fridge stocked and in sync with the kitchen.

## The three strategies — short analogies

- **Cache‑Aside (Lazy Loading)**: Customers check the fridge; if the item isn't there someone asks the kitchen and then puts one in the fridge. Good for read‑heavy, tolerant of cold misses.
- **Write‑Through**: When the kitchen makes a change, the staff immediately update both the fridge and the kitchen, ensuring the fridge is always up to date.
- **Write‑Back (Write‑Behind)**: Staff update the fridge immediately, but batch and flush changes back to the kitchen later. Fast writes, riskier consistency.

## How a request flows (Cache‑Aside) — sequence diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>App: Request for key
  App->>Cache: GET key
  Cache-->>App: MISS
  App->>DB: SELECT key
  DB-->>App: Row
  App->>Cache: SET key (populate)
  App-->>Client: Response
```

## Comparison: Pros, Cons, When to use

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Cache‑Aside | Simple, explicit control, good for irregular writes | Cold misses, potential cache stampede, eventual consistency | Read‑heavy with complex invalidation; when writes are less frequent |
| Write‑Through | Stronger read consistency, simpler for reads | Write latency increases (write to cache + DB), write amplification | Systems that require fresher reads and moderate write volumes |
| Write‑Back | Fast writes (return immediately), fewer DB writes (batch) | Risk of data loss on crash, complex flushing and durability | High write throughput where low latency is critical and risk can be mitigated (e.g., durable write buffer) |

## Before / After (Adding a cache correctly)

| Metric | Before (No cache) | After (Cache‑Aside or Write‑Through) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Average read latency | 50–200 ms | 1–10 ms (cache hit) |
| DB QPS | High (all reads) | Much lower (hits only miss) |
| Consistency complexity | Low (single source) | Medium (need invalidation/updates) |
| Operational cost | High DB cost | Lower DB cost but higher memory/cache cost |
| Failure modes | DB overload/latency spikes | Stale reads, cache stampede, eviction surprises |

## Code examples

Cache‑Aside (Node.js + Redis + Postgres pseudo‑code):

```js
async function getUser(id) {
  const cacheKey = `user:${id}`
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey)
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached)

  // cache miss -> load from DB
  const user = await pg.query('select * from users where id = $1', [id])
  if (user) {
    // set cache with TTL to reduce stale data
    await redis.set(cacheKey, JSON.stringify(user), 'EX', 60 * 5)
  }
  return user
}

// On update, invalidate or update cache
async function updateUser(id, data) {
  await pg.query('update users set ... where id = $1', [id])
  await redis.del(`user:${id}`) // simple invalidation
}
```

Write‑Through (conceptual): every write writes to both cache and DB synchronously.

```js
async function writeUser(id, data) {
  await redis.set(`user:${id}`, JSON.stringify(data))
  await pg.query('update users set ... where id = $1', [id])
}
```

Write‑Back (simplified): update cache and enqueue for background flush.

```js
async function writeUserWriteBack(id, data) {
  // write to cache immediately
  await redis.set(`user:${id}`, JSON.stringify(data))
  // push to write queue for background flush
  await queue.push({ id, data })
}

// background worker
async function flushWorker() {
  while (true) {
    const item = await queue.pop()
    await pg.query('update users set ... where id = $1', [item.id])
  }
}
```

## When to use which (practical rules)

- Use **cache‑aside** for most read‑heavy workloads where writes are less frequent and you can tolerate a small window of staleness. It’s simple and widely used.
- Use **write‑through** when you need fresher reads and want reads to be simple and strongly consistent with cache contents. Accept slightly higher write latency.
- Use **write‑back** only when write latency is critical and you can tolerate complex failure handling, or you have a durable, replicated write buffer to avoid data loss.

## Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

- **Cache stampede (thundering herd)**: many clients miss and hammer the DB. Mitigate with request coalescing, mutexes, randomized TTLs, or early recomputation.
- **Stale reads / invalidation races**: ensure your update path invalidates or updates the cache reliably. Use TTLs, versioning, or compare‑and‑set strategies.
- **Double writes / write amplification**: write‑through doubles write effort; ensure DB and cache writes are idempotent or compensated on failure.
- **Data loss in write‑back**: if the cache/queue crashes, you might lose buffered writes. Use durable queues (e.g., Kafka), replication, or persistent write logs.
- **Eviction surprises**: items evicted from the cache can cause sudden spikes in DB load. Monitor hit rate and set appropriate eviction policies and capacity planning.
- **Transactional consistency**: caches outside DB transactions can see inconsistent views. For strong consistency, consider read‑through caches integrated with transactional middleware or avoid caching transactional hotspots.

## Monitoring & ops checklist

- Track cache hit ratio and miss latency.
- Monitor DB QPS and compare before/after caching rollout.
- Alert on sudden drop in hit rate (eviction or misconfiguration).
- Simulate failovers: what happens if cache becomes unavailable? Does the app fail open to DB?

## TL;DR

Choose cache‑aside for simplicity and read‑heavy workloads; choose write‑through when you need fresher reads at the cost of write latency; choose write‑back only when write latency is critical and you can manage durability risks. Design for stampede protection, proper invalidation, and observability.
keyTakeaways: Cache‑aside is simple and great for read‑heavy workloads, but expect cold misses and need for invalidation.
Write‑through provides fresher cache reads but increases write latency and overhead.
Write‑back yields fastest writes but requires durable buffering and careful flush/consistency handling.
